channel.title: Unfinished Bitness
channel.description: Finishing bits of technology, vintage and new.

item.title: Total Recall
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: Fresh out of the software factory, I have just released another cubeSQL based application for the Atari 8 bit computer! Total Recall is a recall assistant. Say what? Have you ever needed to recall when you last did something? Stuff that isn’t quite calendar worthy. Like, when was the last time I cleaned the curtains? Or, […]
item.content:encoded:
Fresh out of the software factory, I have just released another cubeSQL based application for the Atari 8 bit computer!

Total Recall is a recall assistant. Say what? Have you ever needed to recall when you last did something? Stuff that isn’t quite calendar worthy. Like, when was the last time I cleaned the curtains? Or, when was the last time I washed the car? Total Recall helps you recall those “last times”, right down to the time of day!

After adding an Event to Total Recall, simply Refresh it each time you perform it. Total Recall will show you how many times you’ve completed it, and allow you to see the exact dates and times of each occurrence via a convenient history feature giving you a “Total Recall” of the event! It’s your personal grey matter assistant!

A full manual is included, and there is a video demonstration on the Total Recall software page.

There is dedicated a page for my software on this very blog site. Total Recall can now be found on there.

https://unfinishedbitness.info/total-recall/

item.title: A8 C Library v1.60
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: I just released version 1.60 of my C Library for the Atari 8 bit computers. This version includes the following: -Added StrCln to A8LibStr which removes {} characters from strings. -Added StrRep to A8LibStr which replaces occurrences of a substring with another substring. -Added StrCnt to A8LibStr which counts a given character in a string. -Added StrNChr […]
item.content:encoded:
I just released version 1.60 of my C Library for the Atari 8 bit computers.

This version includes the following:

-Added StrCln to A8LibStr which removes {} characters from strings.

-Added StrRep to A8LibStr which replaces occurrences of a substring with another substring.

-Added StrCnt to A8LibStr which counts a given character in a string.

-Added StrNChr to A8LibStr which find the nth occurrence of a character in a string.

-Added StrTrm to A8LibStr which trims trailing spaces from a string.

-Added IsFName which determines if a filename is valid or not.

-Added A8FujiJSON library.

-Added A8CubeSQL library (base and config).

And of course, updated documentation and additional demo program for cubeSQL called CUBEDEMO.

Find it here:

https://unfinishedbitness.info/c-library/

item.title: One Click Atari!
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: With recent additions to Atari800MacX which now allow Network SIO to communicate with emulated FujiNet devices, I’ve been using it over Altirra running under CrossOver when I can because Atari800MacX is seamless and native. Previously there was a nice graphical FujiNet PC Launcher for macOS titled as such. When starting this older version both the […]
item.content:encoded:
With recent additions to Atari800MacX which now allow Network SIO to communicate with emulated FujiNet devices, I’ve been using it over Altirra running under CrossOver when I can because Atari800MacX is seamless and native.

Previously there was a nice graphical FujiNet PC Launcher for macOS titled as such. When starting this older version both the FujiNet and NetSIO hub are running. Before starting Atari800MacX, the NetSIO Hub needs to be stopped, but is needed for Altirra. This required extra clicks slowing things down when not using Altirra. The author (a8jan) was mentioning on the FujiNet Discord that it would be updated from the 202410 release to bring the firmware in line with current physical devices. I asked if it would be possible to add a config at the same to prevent the NetSIO Hub from starting at startup. My request was answered positively and the newest version is now 202602.

With the new version, I built an Automator workflow as an “Application” that starts the emulated FujiNet with the NetSIO Hub turned off, loads and then starts Atari800MacX with a preset configuration file. Now I can simply click the Automator application from the Finder, and the everything is loaded with one click!

First, create a new Automator Application. You will need to add three steps to it:

1) “Run Shell Script” and use the following as the command. The –args is required to pass the –nohub parameter to the launcher application:

open -a <full-path>/FujiNet-PC-202602/launcher.app --args --nohub

2. “Get Specific Finder Items“. Point it at the Atari800MacX configuration file you want to be loaded. This configuration should have NetSIO enabled, and that is only available on the 6.2+ version of Atari800MacX:

3. “Open Finder Items“, and set it to open Atari800MacX binary.

4. Save it with a name that makes sense for you. I stored them in a “QuickStarts” folder in the Atari800MacX folder and created an alias of that QuickStarts folder on my desktop:

Now, just double click the saved Automator Application!

Bingo! Everything launched in the desired end state with one click!

Enjoy!

item.title: Dr. Love
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: Just in time for Valentines Day, I have released another cubeSQL backed Atari 8 home computer database program. This one is for the adults, and is designed to track your, er, uh, bedroom adventures! Dr. Love will help you track how often, how well your performing, and several other aspects of your love life. It […]
item.content:encoded:
Just in time for Valentines Day, I have released another cubeSQL backed Atari 8 home computer database program. This one is for the adults, and is designed to track your, er, uh, bedroom adventures!

Dr. Love will help you track how often, how well your performing, and several other aspects of your love life. It works much like Fuji Do and Fuji Poo, and full manual is included.

I created a page dedicated to my software on this very blog site recently. Dr. Love can now be found on this site.

https://unfinishedbitness.info/dr-love/

item.title: Atari 8 Bit & SQL
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: Wait, what?!? Around mid 2023 I had an idea to somehow connect the Atari 8 bit to a modern database that could do all the heavy lifting of database related functions which would effectively remove record storage limitations, better indexing, and superior query abilities. This would leave the Atari to performing the UI. I started […]
item.content:encoded:
Wait, what?!?

Around mid 2023 I had an idea to somehow connect the Atari 8 bit to a modern database that could do all the heavy lifting of database related functions which would effectively remove record storage limitations, better indexing, and superior query abilities. This would leave the Atari to performing the UI. I started developing this into something workable in late 2024.

To accomplish my goal I selected cubeSQL as the backend database. cubeSQL essentially wraps SQLite databases as a service which can be interacted with via API using JSON. The first order of business was authenticating to cubeSQL. For this I needed SHA hashing. The Atari just couldn’t do this. When I presented the problem to the FujiNet team, Thom Cherryholmes came through with FujiNet hashing API calls.

After building C routines to send and receive the JSON data, I was able to build an authentication routine. With authentication complete, I moved onto JSON parsing. I stumbled here for long time due to how the cubeSQL result JSON is structured. Then in early 2025 I had an epiphany on how to handle it and wrote my own parsing routines.

With the communications in place, I started on writing my a program for doing basic interactions with cubeSQL. Eventually it grew to performing almost all of the cubeSQLAdmin functions (management of databases, tables, users, group, permissions, etc), and handling CRUD operations. Though CRUD operations are not the primary focus.

Now, I am releasing CubeDot which is a dot prompt style interface to cubeSQL akin to the dot prompt in dBase and FoxPlus. Why a dot prompt? dBase and its followers had a significant impact on me, both personally and professionally. I have always wished there was a dot prompt style program for the Atari 8 bit. And today, for the first time, there is!

I pretty much completed CubeDot in December 2025, and focused on a proof of concept app to demonstrate the cubeSQL bindings I wrote in a real world application form. I had originally created an application back in 2024 using a database engine I wrote from scratch called ADB (Atari DataBase), which loosely followed the xBase format, but it lacked indexing which would limit usefulness in an application. The application was a good candidate to use cubeSQL. I converted this application to use the cubeSQL bindings I wrote. During development of this application, I ran into issues where I would need to rework some of the cubeSQL bindings so they functioned differently. When I had trouble I had a nickname for it, and eventually decided to build the nickname version of the application as well.

In January 2026 I focused on use testing and documentation, and am now ready to release the project. My C library cubeSQL bindings will be released when I complete the documentation.

With the release of CubeDot for cubeSQL management, I am also releasing the original proof of concept application Fuji Do, and the spoof Fuji Poo! Fuji Do is a simple Task Manager, and Fuji Poo is a Poop Logger. Both Fuji Do and Fuji Poo use my C library TUI interface elements, form based record entry, cubeSQL for the database, and offer a browse view allowing for the display of 10 records at a time with forward/reverse navigation that supports tagging, filtering, sort ordering, and sort direction. Fuji Do also has a search function. Fuji Do and Fuji Poo will support up to 100,000 records, technically more, but I capped the record count display at 5 digits. All of this in 48K!

What do you need to use these? In short the following, but see the documentation for details:
-48K Atari computer
-FujiNet
-a cubeSQL instance

Here is an example of doing a select against Fuji Do’s database:

Where can you get these? Right here:

CubeDot (cubeSQL management in dBase dot prompt style) is available here:
https://unfinishedbitness.info/cubedot/

Fuji Do (ToDo Manager) is available here:
https://unfinishedbitness.info/fuji-do/

Fuji Poo (Poop Logger) is available here:
https://unfinishedbitness.info/fuji-poo/

item.title: Mr. Paint
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: Last year I wrote and released an Atari 8 bit ATASCII screen editor called Mr. Paint. I neglected to post about it here, but should have. Without going into too much detail in this post, in short I needed a way to edit static ATASCII screens (not animations), and was not happy with any of […]
item.content:encoded:
Last year I wrote and released an Atari 8 bit ATASCII screen editor called Mr. Paint. I neglected to post about it here, but should have.

Without going into too much detail in this post, in short I needed a way to edit static ATASCII screens (not animations), and was not happy with any of the existing editors I found. So I wrote my own. Mr. Paint is that result.

It has a lot of features such as block operations, a windowed interface, and different save modes.

I created a page dedicated to my software on this very blog site recently. Mr. Paint can now be found here, rather than the Git site I was using prior.

https://unfinishedbitness.info/mr-paint/

media:content.media:title: Mr. Paint Logo drawn in ATASCII with sub-title ATASCII Screen Editor

item.title: Retro Modern Analysis
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: In the past I’ve upgraded my main computer about every 2 years. This last cycle I stretched it to 4 years, primarily because they’ve gotten so fast and capable I haven’t had a need. I could probably stretch it out even further if I really wanted, but I do like to stay somewhat current. My […]
item.content:encoded:
In the past I’ve upgraded my main computer about every 2 years. This last cycle I stretched it to 4 years, primarily because they’ve gotten so fast and capable I haven’t had a need. I could probably stretch it out even further if I really wanted, but I do like to stay somewhat current.

My current primary is an Apple MacBook Pro M1 Max with 10 CPUs and 32 GPUs, and 32 GB ram. A very capable machine by any means. I’ve been thinking about upgrading for the past year, and decided it was time despite how close the M5 may be (or not be with rumored delay until 2026).

To analyze my options, I pulled metric data from GeekBench for each of Apple’s current MacBook Pro M4 Pro and M4 Max configuration lineup. To visualize performance percentage increase over the M1 Max I have, I created a spreadsheet. For fun, I chose to do it with retro software – specifically Quattro Pro 5 running under DosBox-X, in graphic mode.

The answer to my upgrade path became immediately clear. The lower M4 Pro configuration would yield less graphic performance than my M1 Max. The upper M4 Pro configuration would yield just a tiny increase in graphic performance over my M1 Max. These don’t meet the graphic performance largely because they have a much smaller number of GPU’s.

The only viable path for my needs is either of the M4 Max configurations. The lower configuration here yields a 41 to 50% increase in graphic performance. The upper configuration yields a 67 to 73% increase in graphic performance!

It’s’ noteworthy to mention that all the M4 Pro and M4 Max CPU performance metrics show a solid 61% increase in single core mode. The M4 Pro in multi core mode ranges from 63 to 80% increase. The M4 Max in multi core mode ranges from 85 to 106% increase in performance!

I chose to go with the M4 Max in top 16 CPU and 40 GPU configuration. I’m expecting this to last at least another 4 iterations, possibly 5 – looking at you M8 or M9.

When working with Quattro Pro 5, I had to re-learn a bit of formula entry. It was fun to work with software I haven’t touched in 3 decades. There were some things that were not as easy to do as modern spreadsheets, but I was still able to do what I wanted.

Then I thought, what would it be like entering this on an even older spreadsheet? So I broke out LDW Power on the Atari ST. This was very easy to use. The only thing I couldn’t do was apply color to the cells, but otherwise managed to recreate the Quattro Pro work:

At this point, I thought let’s see what this would be like on the Atari 8 bit. So I pulled out Synapse SynCalc and started building it. The limited screen space was problematic and left me wishing it was wider. It was also limited in formatting. No bold, or coloring. I also had to limit the size of column A to fit at least half of the metric columns in one view with the remaining half underneath (still visible). This meant abbreviation in column A. SynCalc did give me some trouble when copying and pasting. I couldn’t get it to copy multiple rows of multiple columns, only a single row of columns. It’s been a while though, so maybe I was just not doing it right, and cells C2 and E2 have an error remnant I neglected to cleanup before taking the screenshot. I still managed to get the analysis though:

Of all three sheets, my preference for ease of use is LDW Power on the Atari ST. I didn’t expect that. I fully expected the experience to get worse as I travelled back in time. The next time you need to build a spreadsheet, consider using some old software!

item.title: Crossing Over
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: Recently I posted about how to get Altirra running on Apple silicon M series Macs using Whisky. In this article I setup Codeweavers CrossOver in a similar fashion as I did for Whisky, on an M series Mac such that there is a 320K Atari XL computer with SpartaDOS X, PC-Link, Real Time 8 cartridge, […]
item.content:encoded:
Recently I posted about how to get Altirra running on Apple silicon M series Macs using Whisky.  In this article I setup Codeweavers CrossOver in a similar fashion as I did for Whisky, on an M series Mac such that there is a 320K Atari XL computer with SpartaDOS X, PC-Link, Real Time 8 cartridge, and FujiNet.  Like the Whisky setup, it’s not a short instruction set, so grab a coffee.

Why the CrossOver article?  The Whisky solution was working well for me, on and off.  Occasionally I would run into issues where Altirra would freeze up at random times including booting, often requiring killing Whisky completely and waiting 30 seconds or so before relaunching.  This got me looking into why.  

I discovered that Whisky, while using Apples Game Porting Tool Kit (GPTK), also uses an older version of Wine.  I also discovered that since I last looked at CrossOver, it uses a newer versions of Wine than Whisky, and many new enhancements including GPTK. So I decided to give it another spin.  After spending some time with CrossOver, I found that it indeed runs more reliably, and seems to run a bit quicker.  This is likely attributed to the newer version of Wine, among other enhancements.  This is a different experience than I had a few years ago.

At a minimum, you need Codeweavers CrossOver for Mac, and the latest Altirra.  You can find each as follows:

CrossOver: https://www.codeweavers.com/crossover

Altirra: https://virtualdub.org/altirra.html

I’m not going to explain how to install either because both are explained well online.  There are a couple of references to the Whisky article I created that you may want, or need, to reference.  Let’s go…

1) Once CrossOver is installed, launch it.  You have an empty instance:

2) From the main menu, select Bottle > New Bottle.  Give it a name like Altirra, and select Windows 10 64 bit:

3) Once the bottle is created, on the right is a button to open the C drive, do it (please ignore my typo on the bottle name, but note if you make a mistake you can simply edit the name in place):

4) Now copy your Atari files over to the C drive window. You’ll need ROMs, and Cartridges, and perhaps some disks.  Alternatively you can point to them later by pointing Altirra at the Y: drive (which is mapped to the host system):

5) From the empty Altirra bottle, click the “Run Command” button in the upper right.  Set the bottle to the Altirra bottle.  Use the Browse button to locate the Altirra executable on the C drive.  Click “Save Command as a Launcher”.  Then click Cancel:

6) This is optional.  On the Altirra bottle, click the “Wine Configuration” under Control Panels on the right.  Navigate to the Graphics tab, and set the screen resolution to something larger than 96 dpi.  The text is real small on a Retina display:

7) Double click the Altirra icon, which is the default launcher.  This will start Altirra.  Since it is the first time setup, you will need to configure it.  There are only a few questions, but you will need to know the location of your ROMs and Cartridges beforehand:

8) Once Altirra is running, and it may be on the Atari Self Test screen, on the main menu navigate to System > Profiles > Edit Profiles.  Select the XL/XE Computer profile.  Then click Add to add a new profile based on it.  For this exercise, name it “SpartaDOS X PCLink RT8 FujiNet”.

9) Select the new profile that was just created.  Check the box labeled “Show in Menu”, then click “Edit Categories”. On the Edit Categories dialog, click the “All” checkbox on the top left, then click the OK button:

10) Now we need to configure the computer.  On the main menu, navigate to System > Configure System, and then on the right, select Firmware in the Computer section.  Assign the Operating System “Atari XL/XE OS ver.3”.  Assign BASIC “Atari BASIC rev. C”:

11) On the same Configure System dialog, we need to add the Real Time 8 cartridge, PC-Link, and FujiNet.  Select Devices in the Peripherals section.  On the right side, select Computer.  Not having this selected can cause CrossOver to crash for some reason.  Then click the Add button.  On the Add device dialog, scroll down to the “Serial I/O bus devices” section, select PCLink, and click OK:

12) Next you need to configure PCLink. Set the host path you want the PCL device to point to using the ellipsis button.  If you want to allow writes, check the box.  Likewise for the timestamps.  Then click the OK button.

13) On the Configure System dialog, again select Computer on the right side, click the Add button, then scroll down to the “Cartridge devices” section, select “R-Time 8”, then click the “OK” button:

14) Now to add FujiNet.  You must have access to the FujiNet-PC driver for Altirra.  Still on the Configure System dialog, again select Computer on the right side, click the Add button, then scroll down to the “Other devices” section, select “Custom device”, then click the OK button:

15) The custom device will prompt you for the config path in a dialog titled “Custom device options”.  Click the ellipses button.  You are now prompted to “Select Custom Device Description”.  Navigate to the folder where your placed the FujiNet-PC driver for Altirra, and select “netsio.atdevice”, then click the Open button:

16) All the devices for this profile have been added, and you will be back at the Configure System dialog.  If FujiNet-PC is not currently running, there will be a red warning and message saying there is no connection:

17) The hardware is now configured.  Time to add SpartaDOS.  On the main menu navigate to File > Attach Cartridge.  On the Load Cartridge dialog, navigate to your ROM file directory, select the “SDX449_sdx128.car” file, and click the Open button:

18) Now we need to assign a boot disk.  This should have the PCLINK.SYS file on it, as well as a CONFIG.SYS and INIDOS.SYS file.  On the main menu, navigate to File > Disk Drives.  On the D1 row, click the ellipses button, navigate to a SpartaDOS X initialized diskette image, select it and click Open.  Now click OK on the Disk Drives dialog:

19) Reboot the Atari by navigating to System > Cold Start on the main menu.  You may get some errors, that’s ok for now.

20) You will likely want to set your CONFIG.SYS as follows, which will load all the drivers needed for this profile.  This can be done using SpartaDOS X builtin command “ED CONFIG.SYS”:

USE BANKED
DEVICE SPARTA
DEVICE ATARIDOS
DEVICE SIO
DEVICE D1:PCLINK.SYS
DEVICE RTIME8

20) Close Altirra using File > Exit on the main menu.

21) Launch FujiNet-PC:

20) Back on the CrossOver bottle for Altirra, you should see one icon for Altirra64:

21) In my previous article on setting this up with Whisky, I created a couple of batch files that would launch Altirra directly into specific profiles. I reuse them here:

22) The batch files need to have the path to the Altirra binary updated.  The CrossOver Altirra bottle is a different location than the Whisky bottle.  Find the path to the CrossOver Altirra bottle, and update the batch files, while ensuring the profile name matches what was defined in the previous steps:

23) Back on the CrossOver Altirra bottle screen, click on “Run Command” again in the upper right.  Navigate to the batch file that matches the profile that was just setup, select it and Click “Open”:

24) Instead of clicking Run, click on “Save Command as a shortcut”, then click the Cancel button.

25) Back on the CrossOver Altirra bottle screen, you can see the newly defined shortcut which appears as a gears icon, and has the name of the batch file:

26) Double click it to launch!  As a reminder, the batch file has /tempprofile added to the command line so changes you make while Altirra is running won’t be save or damage the profile:

Well, that’s another 26 step journey.  By now you should be an expert at setting up Altirra with configuration specific profiles on an Apple silicon M series Mac. 

Have you played Atari today?

item.title: Altirra, Profiles, FujiNet, M Silicon Mac, and You
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: If you have a Mac with an Apple Silicon M series processor, you have no troubles running your favorite Atari 8 bit programs using Atari800MacX – until you need to use an unsupported device such as FujiNet. This use case necessitates using Altirra, and FujiNet-PC. Altirra is a Windows native application, and to run that […]
item.content:encoded:
If you have a Mac with an Apple Silicon M series processor, you have no troubles running your favorite Atari 8 bit programs using Atari800MacX – until you need to use an unsupported device such as FujiNet. This use case necessitates using Altirra, and FujiNet-PC.

Altirra is a Windows native application, and to run that on an M silicon Mac, you need an “emulation” layer. There are few flavors of Wine that can do the job, some with more success than others – Wine, Crossover, and Whisky. Whisky is dedicated to running on M silicon Macs, and utilizes Apples Game Porting Toolkit to help it run efficiently.

However, using Altirra can be troublesome for Mac users, or those otherwise uninitiated in its use. Altirra has a feature called Profiles which I’ll discuss first as it’s pivotal in using multiple configurations of hardware in Altirra. Profiles allow you to customize the virtual Atari machine and accessories it is executing. There are some important tricks to ensure that once you have a profile setup, you don’t change it inadvertently simply by mounting or unmounting a disk, or adding a device such as an Atari 850 Interface, etc. By default, Altirra will update the active profile as you make changes. You will make (or have made) mistakes in this regard, and it is very frustrating when you select the profile in the future and the configuration is not as expected. Ooof!

This article will guide you through setting up, and successfully using Altirra Profiles, and FujiNet-PC with Altirra, as well as setting up shortcuts (pins) in Whisky to launch the profiles directly, while preserving them so any changes you make during a session won’t alter the profile.

First you need Whisky. It can be installed two ways. One is from the website at:
https://github.com/Whisky-App/Whisky

The other method is via Brew with this command at the Terminal:

brew install --cask whisky

Once Whisky is installed, install the latest version of Altirra into a new Whisky bottle. This guide won’t go into the details of installation and setup of Altirra. Altirra can be obtained from the website at:
https://virtualdub.org/altirra.html

Here is one hint that will help you. In Whisky, select your new Altirra bottle, then click the “Open C: Drive” button which will open a Finder window where you can copy the Altirra zip file to. I find it easier to unzip it first, then copy the entire zip directory to a folder in that Finder window. You will also want to copy any Cartridges, Disks, OS ROMs, etc that you want to use with Altirra. If you don’t want to duplicate these files, you can open Terminal and create a symlink (alias) in that c_drive folder that points to another location in your Mac. Symlinks can be setup with the command:

ln -s /destination/path /source/path

Pause and take a breath. Before going further, if you want to use FujiNet with Altirra, you also need FujiNet-PC. There is a nice all-in-one launcher package available courtesy of Jan Krupa (a8jan) on GitHub. You need a build of at least 2024.10 compiled and bundled for MacOS. The file you need is “fujinet-pc-bundle_2410.01_macos-13.tgz” and can be found from the Launcher website here:
https://github.com/a8jan/fujinet-pc-launcher/releases

Once Altirra is installed and setup such that an Atari computer boots to Memopad or Self Test, you are ready to proceed. In the next short 26 easy A to Z steps, you’ll be switching between profiles in Altirra with a single launch from Whisky like a boss, and even using FujiNet-PC.

A) With an Atari booted, it doesn’t matter to what (DOS, Memopad, etc), select “System > Profiles > Edit Profiles”:

B) Select the base machine profile you want to start from by left clicking on it. Then click the “Add” button. It will have the name “” and be highlighted:

C) Set the name to whatever you want. It makes sense to specify components that you are going to set in the profile. Here, I’ve used the name “SpartaDOS X PCLink FujiNet”. After accepting the entry, you may want to also click the “Show in Menu” option, which will allow the Profile to be selected from the Profiles menu:

D) With the new profile selected, click the “Edit Categories” button. This is very important. On the “Edit Profile Categories” dialog, check the “(All)” checkbox, then click the OK button:

E) Select System > Profiles > and the name of your new profile from the menu, in my case “SpartaDOS X PCLink FujiNet”:

F) Now add all the cartridges and disks you want this machine profile to have. Here I am adding SpartaDOS X v4.49 cartridge:

G) Here I am adding SpartaDOS boot disk into drive 1, which contains the ini, config, and autoexec files and some utilities on it:

H) Then set the hardware configuration you want the machine profile to have. Select System > Configure System from the menu:

I) The Configure System dialog has a lot of options. One of the you’ll want to visit is the Firmware section. Here you will set the OS version and BASIC version:

J) You’ll also want to visit the Memory section where you can set how much memory you want to be available:

K) Next go to the Devices section. Here you will add expansion devices and peripherals to the emulated Atari:

L) Select Computer on the right side, then click the “Add…” button. From the Add Device dialog, find and add the device you want. Here I am adding PCLink:

M) Some devices require some additional configuration. PCLink is one of those. Here I set the location of the host operating systems folder I want shared via PCLink:

N) Next I’m adding the Real Time 8 cartridge. I didn’t name the profile with it, but did change the name of the profile to reflect this addition later on. Real Time 8 is in the Cartridge Devices section with the name “R-Time 8”, and I use the abbreviation RT8 in my profile name:

O) Now you may be saying, but I didn’t see FujiNet in the device list. Your right! You add it through the Other Devices section with the device named “Custom device”:

P) Like PCLink, custom devices have a secondary configuration. For FujiNet you will need to have the FujiNet-PC driver which should be part of the FujiNet-PC package, and you will of course have to copy these files to the Altirra Whisky bottles C drive. Set the config path of the custom device for FujiNet-PC to that location and select the “netsio.atdevice” file:

You will see a connection error on the device name, unless you have previous launched FujiNet-PC. This is OK.

Intermission!

Good news, you’re over half way done, ready to boot, and do some final configuration!

Q) If you added FujiNet to your profile, launch FujiNet-PC before proceeding. You will know it launched when you see a graphical representation of an actual FujiNet device appear on the screen.

Here is a helpful hint. Right click on the image, and select “Open WebUI” to open the FujiNet configuration web page. In it you can set emulated printers, mount disks, tapes, etc.

R) Time for the big test! From the Altirra menu, select System > Cold Reset to reboot the Atari:

S) If all went well, the Atari will have booted and initialized all the devices. You should be able to see PCLink config and SpartaDOS. Slightly out of order, but this screenshot doesn’t show the Real Time 8 cartridge initializing because I hadn’t added it yet:

T) Now comes a very, very important setting! To prevent Altirra from modifying your new profile as you swap disks and cartridges, etc, you need to tell it the profile is temporary. Kind of a dumb trick. Select System > Profiles > Temporary Profile.

To prove the profile is saved, switch to another profile, change a disk, then switch back to your new profile. It should be intact.

U) With Altirra and Altirra Profiles working, let’s move on to Whisky and make it easier to launch directly into an Altirra Profile. You probably will want to tell Whisky to launch your primary Altirra instance with Temporary Profile enabled. Do this by right clicking the “pin” (icon) for the primary Altirra executable, select Config, then add “/tempprofile” to the Arguments parameter. Once done, click the < button to return to the Altirra Whisky bottle:

Whisky only lets you create one pin per executable, so we can’t pin Altirra64.exe with different parameters for each profile. To get around this, create a MS-DOS batch file, stored in the Altirra Whisky bottle C drive, with the command to launch Altirra with profile you want launched at boot. I named the batch file after the Altirra Profile. Example: SpartaDOSXPCLinkRT8FujiNet.bat

The contents of the batch file will be a single line with the full host OS path to the Altirra executable in the Whisky bottle C drive followed by the “/profile” switch which specifies the name of the profile you want Altirra to activate, and most importantly, also followed by “/tempprofile”. Here are the contents of two batch files I created, one with FujiNet, and one without.

Without FujiNet:

/Users/wave/Whisky/64B7ACEE-4D60-4035-B093-3971F4E23042/drive_c/Altirra-4.21/Altirra64.exe /profile:"SpartaDOS X PCLink RT8" /tempprofile

With FujiNet:

/Users/wave/Whisky/64B7ACEE-4D60-4035-B093-3971F4E23042/drive_c/Altirra-4.21/Altirra64.exe /profile:"SpartaDOS X PCLink RT8 FujiNet" /tempprofile

V) Now that you have batch files created for each profile, it’s time to make pins for them in Whisky. With the Altirra Whisky bottle selected, click the + icon with a circle around it to add a new pin. Give the pin a name. I used the same name as the profile. One caveat is longer names will be truncated and show an ellipses so you may need to shorten them with more abbreviations. Click the Browse button by the Program Path field, navigate to the Altirra Whisky bottle C drive and folder you stored the batch file in, then select the batch file:

W) Edit the new pin by right clicking it and choosing Config, then set the Argument to “/tempprofile”, and click the < button to return to the Altirra Whisky bottle:

X) OK, you’re all set and ready to launch! That wasn’t so bad, was it? Double click on the profile pin you want to launch:

Y) Within a few seconds you should see your Atari machine running in Altirra with all the configured drives, and peripherals at the go. Remember, anytime you are going to use FujiNet, make sure you launch the FujiNet-PC host application first, then the Altirra pin/profile that uses it.

There is one small side effect to launching pins that point to a batch file, which you can see in that last screenshot. You can see the command line window that runs the batch file as a separate window behind the Altirra window. Don’t worry though, it closes when you exit.

Some of you may be thinking there is a potentially easier way to use the batch file method. There is, but it is slightly more complex. I kept it simpler for the article.

Z) As a reminder to myself, I created a text file on my SpartDOS boot disk that has hints on key mappings for special Atari keystrokes. I then added a line in the “AUTOEXEC.BAT” file on that disk to display it, so at boot I get a reminder of the keystrokes to use for Start, etc. To display the file, add 

TYPE filename.txt

at the end of the “AUTOEXEC.BAT” file. Here is my result:

There you go, from a literal A to Z in steps! I hope this helps you get Altirra and FujiNet running on your Apple M silicon processor Mac, and using Altirra profiles proficiently and safely!

item.title: A8 C Library v1.50
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: I just released version 1.50 of my C Library. This version includes the following: -Added RAW2INT(). Converts raw scan code to internal code.-Added GConf(). Presents confirmation prompt and accepts Yes/No.-Added GAlertM(). Presents alert with severity as window ornament (Info, Warning, Error). Deprecated GAlert() with preprocessor translation to GAlertM().-Added StrTrm(), Trims trailing right spaces from string.-Bug fix in […]
item.content:encoded:
I just released version 1.50 of my C Library.

This version includes the following:

-Added RAW2INT(). Converts raw scan code to internal code.
-Added GConf(). Presents confirmation prompt and accepts Yes/No.
-Added GAlertM(). Presents alert with severity as window ornament (Info, Warning, Error). Deprecated GAlert() with preprocessor translation to GAlertM().
-Added StrTrm(), Trims trailing right spaces from string.
-Bug fix in Menu(). Keystroke variable incorrectly declared as byte, now set as word. Prevents console keys from presenting as normal keys due to overflow.

And of course, updated documentation.

Find it here:

https://unfinishedbitness.info/c-library/

item.title: A8 C Library v1.21
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: I didn’t publicize the C version of my Atari 8 bit library when I released it, except in a small-ish community. The current version is 1.21. The C version of the library includes some things the Action! version does not. The C version has the following features: The library can be downloaded from here where the Action! version […]
item.content:encoded:
I didn’t publicize the C version of my Atari 8 bit library when I released it, except in a small-ish community. The current version is 1.21. The C version of the library includes some things the Action! version does not. The C version has the following features:

  • Text windowing
  • Window gadgets
  • Menu controls
  • FujiNet bindings
  • FujiNet Base64 and Hash bindings
  • APE Time support

The library can be downloaded from here where the Action! version of the library is also hosted. Another Atari enthusiast also converted the library to Pascal, and added a few functions. The Pascal code is hosted here as well.

Rather than post any of it here, I’ll point you at the GitHub page for all versions of the library.

It can be found here:

https://unfinishedbitness.info/c-library/

Go write something cool!

item.title: C Style! Numeric Data in Files
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: I’ve been working on a project where I need to store bytes and integers in a file and read them back in later. The bytes were easy enough, the integers were easy too, once I found the best way. I’m only talking about words consisting of two bytes. The first method I tried was using fprintf() and […]
item.content:encoded:
I’ve been working on a project where I need to store bytes and integers in a file and read them back in later. The bytes were easy enough, the integers were easy too, once I found the best way. I’m only talking about words consisting of two bytes.

The first method I tried was using fprintf() and fscanf(). While this works, fscanf() needs to know how many bytes to read or a terminator. This is also wasteful, as the number is saved in ASCII form. So, a 5 digit number which takes 2 bytes of memory, ends up as 5 bytes plus a terminator once saved with fprintf().

The next method I tried was calculating the high (most significant) and low (least significant) bytes, then storing them as individual bytes using fputc(). This worked well using fgetc() to retrieve the values. The method was efficient storage wise, but came at the cost of mathmatical operations on the save and read.

The last method I tried, which turns out to be the best, is using fwrite() and fread(). In the past I primarily related these to writing and reading arrays of bytes, such as character strings, or numeric arrays. It was perhaps some knowledge I lost over the years, and just re-learned. I discovered using this method its easy to store and retrieve an integer in the storage efficient 2 byte form.

I wrote a small program to set a byte and integer, display them in various forms, write them to file, then read them back into new variables, and finally display them back in the same forms. This is done on the Atari 8 bit using CC65. Compilation instructions are in the header notes.

Here is the short program:

// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Program: numsave.c
// Desc...: Numberic write to/read from file tests
// Author.: Ripdubski
// Date...: 20220809
// Notes..: cl65 -v -t atari numsave.c -o numsave.xex
// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Pull in include files
#include <stdio.h>
#include <stdlib.h>
#include <conio.h>
#include <atari.h>


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void main(void)
// Desc...: Main routine
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void main(void)
{
    // Declare vars
    FILE *fp;
    char c = 65, cb;                     // A, 0x41
    unsigned int i = 32527, ia, ib, ir;  // 0x7F0F

    // Setup screen
    clrscr();
    printf("-[CC65 Numeric Save To File Tests]------");

    // Show char array and address
    printf("\nVariables:\n");
    printf("    Byte: c=%c d=%d\n", c, c);
    printf("     Int: d=%d x=%04x\n", i, i);
    printf("Int HiLo: Hi d=%d x=%02x Lo d=%d x=%02x\n", i/256, i/256, i%256, i%256);

    // Open file
    fp = fopen("numsave.dat", "wb");

    if (fp != NULL) {
        // Write data to file
        printf("\nWrite: fputc byte");
        fputc(c, fp);
        printf(", fwrite int");
        fwrite(&i, 1, sizeof(i), fp);
        printf(", hi/lo.\n");
        fprintf(fp, "%c%c", i/256, i%256);

        // Close file
        fclose(fp);
    }
    else {
        printf("ERROR: Could not create file!\n");
    }

    // Open file
    fp = fopen("numsave.dat", "rb");

    if (fp != NULL) {
        // Read data from file
        printf(" Read: fgetc byte");
        cb = fgetc(fp);
        printf(", fread int");
        fread(&ir, 1, sizeof(ir), fp);
        printf(", hi/lo.\n");
        ia = fgetc(fp);
        ib = fgetc(fp);

        printf("\n    Byte: c=%c d=%d\n", cb, cb);
        printf("     Int: d=%d h=%x\n", ir, ir);
        printf("Int HiLo: Hi d=%d x=%02x Lo d=%d x=%02x\n", ia, ia, ib, ib);
        printf("Calc Int: d=%d h=%04x\n", (ia*256)+ib, (ia*256)+ib);

        // Close file
        fclose(fp);
    }
    else {
        printf("ERROR: Could not open file!\n");
    }

    // Exit
    return;
}

Here is a screen shot of it working, with a hexdump of the file:

item.title: FujiNet Time in C (CC65)
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: FujiNet is a fantastic device for the Atari 8 bit line of computers which provides WiFi, and SD card storage among other things, all over SIO. One of the functions provided is APETIME support, a protocol developed by AtariMax as part of their Atari Peripheral Emulator (APE). In the last post I demonstrated my BASIC […]
item.content:encoded:
FujiNet is a fantastic device for the Atari 8 bit line of computers which provides WiFi, and SD card storage among other things, all over SIO.  One of the functions provided is APETIME support, a protocol developed by AtariMax as part of their Atari Peripheral Emulator (APE).

In the last post I demonstrated my BASIC and Action! versions of a program to get the time from the FujiNet device using APETIME. The procedure is documented here (https://github.com/FujiNetWIFI/fujinet-platformio/wiki/Accessing-the-Real-Time-Clock).  At the time of this writing there was no complete C example, only an example of the call.

After completing the conversion of my Action! library to C, and starting a new project where I needed to get the time from FujiNet from C, I thought it would be a good time to create a version of the FujiNet time program I had written in BASIC and Action!.  This post details my solution.  I wrote the routine into a function which can be called from the main program at any point.  It only requires passing a pointer to a  6 byte array.

To call the SIO routine, I used inline assembly to execute the jump.

As with my Action! source, the routine that gets the time from FujiNet is the FNGTime() function (FujiNet Get Time).  It first sets up the SIO call by assigning appropriate values into the DCB (Device Control Block) memory location.  Using CC65 v2.19+ libraries allows setting the DCB via equates using an OS structure as defined in “atari.h” include file, with no need for the Poke function.  It sets the buffer location (DBUF), to the address of the byte array passed in.

Last it calls the SIO vector using the SIOV function.

The main routine sets aside 6 bytes of storage, calls the FNGTime() function with the address of the storage array, then prints the result in a friendly fashion.

// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Program: fujitime.c
// Desc...: Gets date/time from FujiNet
// Author.: Ripdubski
// Date...: 2023.02.09
// Notes..: cl65 -v [-O] -t atarixl fujitime.c -o fujitime.xex
// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Pull in include files
#include <stdio.h>
#include <atari.h>
#include <conio.h>
#include <unistd.h>

// -----------------------------------
// Proc..: void SIOV(void)
// Desc..: OS SIO Vector
// -----------------------------------
void SIOV(void)
{
    __asm__ ("JSR $E459");
}

// -----------------------------------
// Proc..: void FNGTime(char *bA)
// Desc..: Get date/time from FujiNet
// via APETIME protocol
// Return: 6 bytes DMYHMS into bA
// -----------------------------------
void FNGTime(char *bA)
{
    // Setup SIO call using byte array address
    // by putting values into the DCB.
    // APETime=Device 69 ($45), Unit 1
    // Time command=147 ($93)
    // Get 6 bytes, timeout just over 15s
    OS.dcb.ddevic = 0x45;
    OS.dcb.dunit  = 0x01;
    OS.dcb.dcomnd = 0x93;
    OS.dcb.dstats = 0x40;
    OS.dcb.dbuf   = (void *) bA;
    OS.dcb.dtimlo = 15;
    OS.dcb.dunuse = 0;
    OS.dcb.dbyt   = (unsigned int) 6;

    // Call SIO
    SIOV();
}

// -----------------------------------
// Main Routine
// -----------------------------------
void main(void)
{
    // Storage for 6 bytes preset to 0
    char bDT[7]={ 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0 };

    // Call time routine with the storage array
    FNGTime(bDT);

    // What time is it?
    printf("\nDate: 20%d.%d.%d\n", bDT[2], bDT[1], bDT[0]);
    printf("Time: %d:%d:%d\n", bDT[3], bDT[4], bDT[5]);

    // Wait for a keystroke
    printf("Press any key to continue...\n");
    while (! kbhit()) {};
}

I’m going to include the FNGTime() function in my C library which was converted from my Action! library in 2022.  This will happen in a future release.

item.title: C Style! – Action! Library Conversion 10
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: All the gadgets up until now have been mildly user interactive. In this post I finally tackle the biggest, and likely most useful, gadget – GInput(). This is the string input routine that allows type restricted (numeric, alphabetic, alpha-numeric, or any text) string input. The string editing size can be smaller than the string itself […]
item.content:encoded:
All the gadgets up until now have been mildly user interactive. In this post I finally tackle the biggest, and likely most useful, gadget – GInput(). This is the string input routine that allows type restricted (numeric, alphabetic, alpha-numeric, or any text) string input. The string editing size can be smaller than the string itself and the string will scroll left or right as the cursor is moved. There are some special keys tossed in to get to start of the string, end of the string, clearing the string, inserting and deleting characters. At the time of this writing, the C version hasn’t been fully tested for the last three cases mentioned, but will be by the time the library is published.

The code for GInput() is quite large in relation to the other gadgets, but it does quite a bit.

Here is a demo program showing how the gadget is called:

// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Program: ginput.c
// Desc...: A8 Library Input Test
// Author.: Ripdubski
// Date...: 202208
// Notes..: cl65 -v [-O] -t atarixl ginput.c -o ginput.xex
// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Pull in include files
#include <stdio.h>
#include <conio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <atari.h>

#include "a8defines.h"
#include "a8defwin.h"
#include "a8libmisc.c"
#include "a8libstr.c"
#include "a8libwin.c"
#include "a8libgadg.c"
#include "a8libmenu.c"


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void DoInput(void)
// Desc...: Displays input demo
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void DoInput(void)
{
    byte bW;
    unsigned char cA[41], cB[41], cC[41], cD[41];

    // Assign default string values
    strcpy(cA,  "-100.00                                 ");
    strcpy(cB,  "This string has something to edit in it!");
    strcpy(cC,  "                                        ");
    sprintf(cD, "%cAny character string!%c                 ", CHBALL, CHBALL);

    // Open window & draw form
    bW = WOpen(2, 6, 36, 10, WOFF);
    WOrn(bW, WPTOP, WPLFT, "Input Test");

    WPrint(bW, 1, 1, WOFF, "Data Fields");
    WPrint(bW, 2, 3, WOFF, "Numer:");
    WPrint(bW, 2, 4, WOFF, "Alpha:");
    WPrint(bW, 2, 5, WOFF, "AlNum:");
    WPrint(bW, 2, 6, WOFF, "Any..:");

    // Display fields as is 
    // WPrint will truncate what doesn't fit (27 chars for this window).
    WPrint(bW, 8, 3, WOFF, cA);
    WPrint(bW, 8, 4, WOFF, cB);
    WPrint(bW, 8, 5, WOFF, cC);
    WPrint(bW, 8, 6, WOFF, cD);

    // Input each in succession
    // 27 is the maximum width to display of the 40 lengths
    GInput(bW, 8, 3, GNUMER, 27, cA);
    GInput(bW, 8, 4, GALPHA, 27, cB);
    GInput(bW, 8, 5, GALNUM, 27, cC);
    GInput(bW, 8, 6, GANY,   27, cD);

    // Wait for a key
    WPrint(bW, WPCNT, 8, WOFF, "press any key");
    WaitKCX(WOFF);

    // Close window
    WClose(bW);
}


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void main(void)
// Desc...: Main routine
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void main(void)
{
    // Variables
    byte bW1, bW2, bC, bD;
    unsigned char *pcM[9] =
      { " Alert        ", " Progress     ", " Buttons      ", " Checkboxes   ", " Radio Button ", " Spinner      ", " Input        ", " Quit         " };

    // Setup screen
    WInit();
    WBack(14);

    // Open windows
    bW1 = WOpen(0, 0, 40, 3, WON);
    WPrint(bW1, WPCNT, 1, WOFF, "CC65 A8 Library Test");
    bW2 = WOpen(12, 6, 16, 12, WOFF);
    WOrn(bW2, WPTOP, WPCNT, "Menu");

    // Set done to false
    bD = FALSE;

    // Loop until done (Quit selected)
    while (! bD) {
        // Call menu
        bC = MenuV(bW2, 1, 2, WON, 1, 8, pcM);

        // Process choice
        switch (bC)
        {
            case 1: GAlert("Alert! menu option 1 selected!");
                    break;

            case 2: GAlert("Alert! menu option 2 selected!");
                    break;

            case 3: GAlert("Alert! menu option 3 selected!");
                    break;

            case 4: GAlert("Alert! menu option 4 selected!");
                    break;

            case 5: GAlert("Alert! menu option 5 selected!");
                    break;

            case 6: GAlert("Alert! menu option 6 selected!");
                    break;

            case 7: DoInput();
                    break;

            case 8: bD = TRUE;
                    break;
        }
    }

    // Wait for a keypress
    while (! kbhit()) { };

    // Close windows
    WClose(bW2);
    WClose(bW1);

    // Exit
    return;
}

Here is a screenshot of it in use (the cursor is active on the s in the word has of the Alpha field):

item.title: C Style! – Action! Library Conversion 9
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: This time I cover the conversion of the numeric spinner input. It allows for a number to be chosen where the user does not enter it directly, but rather changes the value by pressing keys representing increment and decrement. It only allows for value between 0 and 250. Values above 250 are reserved for form […]
item.content:encoded:
This time I cover the conversion of the numeric spinner input. It allows for a number to be chosen where the user does not enter it directly, but rather changes the value by pressing keys representing increment and decrement. It only allows for value between 0 and 250. Values above 250 are reserved for form control, such as ESC and TAB. I feel like this is fine since no one will want to cycle up/down into thousands or more, and most likely this will be used from 0 to 100. I contemplated a top limit of 100, but decided to allow up to 250. This was also the last gadget I wrote for my Action! library.

In the demo program, the user is allowed to change the Value field from 5 to 100. Once accepted, the new value is displayed below in the New field.

Here is the demo program showing how its called:

// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Program: gspin.c
// Desc...: A8 Library Window Menu Test
// Author.: Ripdubski
// Date...: 202208
// Notes..: cl65 -v [-O] -t atarixl gspin.c -o gspin.xex
// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Pull in include files
#include <stdio.h>
#include <conio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <atari.h>

#include "a8defines.h"
#include "a8defwin.h"
#include "a8libmisc.c"
#include "a8libstr.c"
#include "a8libwin.c"
#include "a8libgadg.c"
#include "a8libmenu.c"


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void DoSpin(void)
// Desc...: Displays spinner demo
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void DoSpin(void)
{
    byte bW, bC, bV;
    unsigned char cL[4];

    // Set default value
    bV = 10;

    // Open window
    bW = WOpen(8, 7, 24, 9, WOFF);
    WOrn(bW, WPTOP, WPLFT, "Spinner");
    WPrint(bW, 2, 2, WOFF, "Value:");
    WPrint(bW, 4, 4, WOFF,   "New:");

    // Display spinner and get returned value
    // Min value=5, max value=100
    bC = GSpin(bW, 8, 2, 5, 100, bV);

    // What was returned?
    if (bC == XESC)
    {
        GAlert(" Escaped Out! ");
    }
    else if (bC == XTAB)
    {
        GAlert(" Tabbed Out! ");
    }
    else
    {
        // Show the new value
        sprintf(cL, "%3d", bC);
        WPrint(bW, 8, 4, WOFF, cL);
    }

    // Wait for a key
    WPrint(bW, WPCNT, 6, WOFF, "Press any key.");
    WaitKCX(WOFF);

    // Close window
    WClose(bW);
}


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void main(void)
// Desc...: Main routine
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void main(void)
{
    // Variables
    byte bW1, bW2, bC, bD;
    unsigned char *pcM[8] =
      { " Alert        ", " Progress     ", " Buttons      ", " Checkboxes   ", " Radio Button ", " Spinner      ", " Quit         " };

    // Setup screen
    WInit();
    WBack(14);

    // Open windows
    bW1 = WOpen(0, 0, 40, 3, WON);
    WPrint(bW1, WPCNT, 1, WOFF, "CC65 A8 Library Test");
    bW2 = WOpen(12, 6, 16, 11, WOFF);
    WOrn(bW2, WPTOP, WPCNT, "Menu");

    // Set done to false
    bD = FALSE;

    // Loop until done (Quit selected)
    while (! bD) {
        // Call menu
        bC = MenuV(bW2, 1, 2, WON, 1, 7, pcM);

        // Process choice
        switch (bC)
        {
            case 1: GAlert("Alert! menu option 1 selected!");
                    break;

            case 2: GAlert("Alert! menu option 2 selected!");
                    break;

            case 3: GAlert("Alert! menu option 3 selected!");
                    break;

            case 4: GAlert("Alert! menu option 4 selected!");
                    break;

            case 5: GAlert("Alert! menu option 5 selected!");
                    break;

            case 6: DoSpin();
                    break;

            case 7: bD = TRUE;
                    break;
        }
    }

    // Wait for a keypress
    while (! kbhit()) { };

    // Close windows
    WClose(bW2);
    WClose(bW1);

    // Exit
    return;
}

Here is a screenshot of its use:

item.title: C Style! – Action! Library Conversion 8
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: In this post I set out to convert the checkbox routine GCheck(). This displays a checkbox, and allows the user to check or uncheck it. Here is the demo program showing how to call it: Here is a screenshot of it in use:
item.content:encoded:
In this post I set out to convert the checkbox routine GCheck(). This displays a checkbox, and allows the user to check or uncheck it.

Here is the demo program showing how to call it:

// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Program: gcheck.c
// Desc...: A8 Library Gadget Checkbox Test
// Author.: Ripdubski
// Date...: 202208
// Notes..: cl65 -v [-O] -t atarixl gcheck.c -o gcheck.xex
// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Pull in include files
#include <stdio.h>
#include <conio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <atari.h>

#include "a8defines.h"
#include "a8defwin.h"
#include "a8libmisc.c"
#include "a8libstr.c"
#include "a8libwin.c"
#include "a8libgadg.c"
#include "a8libmenu.c"


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void DoCheck(void)
// Desc...: Displays checkbox demo
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void DoCheck(void)
{
    byte bW, bD, bF;
    // Vars used for selections and previous selections
    byte bca, bcb, bcc, bcap, bcbp, bccp;

    // Set defaults for previous selections (initial item status)
    bcap = GCOFF;
    bcbp = GCOFF;
    bccp = GCON;

    // Open window
    bW = WOpen(9, 10, 22, 6, WOFF);
    WOrn(bW, WPTOP, WPLFT, "Checkboxes");
    WPrint(bW, 1, 1, WOFF, "Select your weapons:");
    WPrint(bW, 6, 2, WOFF, "Action!");
    WPrint(bW, 6, 3, WOFF, "BASIC");
    WPrint(bW, 6, 4, WOFF, "C");

    // Display only with previous/default/initial value
    bF = GCheck(bW, 2, 2, GDISP, bcap);
    bF = GCheck(bW, 2, 3, GDISP, bcbp);
    bF = GCheck(bW, 2, 4, GDISP, bccp);

    GAlert(" Check all and TAB to quit. ");

    // Continue until exited
    bF = FALSE;
    while (! bF)
    {
        // Stay on this check until ESC, TAB
        bD = FALSE;
        while (! bD)
        {
            // Call checkbox with edit and previous value
            bca = GCheck(bW, 2, 2, GEDIT, bcap);

            // Check returned value
            if ((bca == XESC) || (bca == XTAB))
            {
                // Allow exit of loop for this checkbox
                bD = TRUE;
            }
            else
            {
                // Set previous value to the current value
                bcap = bca;
            }
        }

        // Stay on this check until ESC, TAB
        bD = FALSE;
        while (! bD)
        {
            // Call checkbox with edit and previous value
            bcb = GCheck(bW, 2, 3, GEDIT, bcbp);

            // Check returned value
            if ((bcb == XESC) || (bcb == XTAB))
            {
                // Allow exit of loop for this checkbox
                bD = TRUE;
            }
            else
            {
                // Set previous value to the current value
                bcbp = bcb;
            }
        }

        // Stay on this check until ESC, TAB
        bD = FALSE;
        while (! bD)
        {
            // Call checkbox with edit and previous value
            bcc = GCheck(bW, 2, 4, GEDIT, bccp);

            // Check returned value
            if ((bcc == XESC) || (bcc == XTAB))
            {
                // Allow exit of loop for this checkbox
                bD = TRUE;
            }
            else
            {
                // Set previous value to the current value
                bccp = bcc;
            }
        }

        // Leave loop wonce all checked - DEMO
        if ((bcap == GCON) && (bcbp == GCON) && (bccp == GCON))
        {
            bF = TRUE;
        }
    }

    GAlert(" All options checked! ");

    // Close window
    WClose(bW);

    return;
}


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void main(void)
// Desc...: Main routine
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void main(void)
{
    // Variables
    byte bW1, bW2, bC, bD;
    unsigned char *pcM[7] = { " Alert        ", " Progress     ", " Buttons      ", " Checkboxes   ", " Radio Button ", " Quit         " };

    // Setup screen
    WInit();
    WBack(14);

    // Open windows
    bW1 = WOpen(0, 0, 40, 3, WON);
    WPrint(bW1, WPCNT, 1, WOFF, "CC65 A8 Library Test");
    bW2 = WOpen(12, 6, 16, 10, WOFF);
    WOrn(bW2, WPTOP, WPCNT, "Menu");

    // Set done to false
    bD = FALSE;

    // Loop until done (Quit selected)
    while (! bD) {
        // Call menu
        bC = MenuV(bW2, 1, 2, WON, 1, 6, pcM);

        // Process choice
        switch (bC)
        {
            case 1: GAlert("Alert! menu option 1 selected!");
                    break;

            case 2: GAlert("Alert! menu option 2 selected!");
                    break;

            case 3: GAlert("Alert! menu option 3 selected!");
                    break;

            case 4: DoCheck();
                    break;

            case 5: GAlert("Alert! menu option 5 selected!");
                    break;

            case 6: bD = TRUE;
                    break;
        }
    }

    // Wait for a keypress
    while (! kbhit()) { };

    // Close windows
    WClose(bW2);
    WClose(bW1);

    // Exit
    return;
}

Here is a screenshot of it in use:

item.title: C Style! – Action! Library Conversion 7
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: With a basic button set now working, I set my sights on converting my Action! library radio button routine. This is the GRadio() function. It displays a set of radio buttons either horizontally or vertically. I made a minor alteration in how navigation/selection is handled in the C version. In the Action! version, space would […]
item.content:encoded:
With a basic button set now working, I set my sights on converting my Action! library radio button routine. This is the GRadio() function. It displays a set of radio buttons either horizontally or vertically. I made a minor alteration in how navigation/selection is handled in the C version. In the Action! version, space would establish a button as selected but not exit until Enter was pressed. After using it a bit and never having been completely satisfied, I chose to have Space select and exit. Navigation feels smoother with this minor change.

To enable form drawing before activating any controls, it is necessary to call the function with a flag telling it to only display the choices and exit. You call it again with an edit flag when it is ready to be activated. After a radio button is edited, it should be called again with the display only flag to clean up – meaning to revert the selection if ESC was pressed, or show the new selection. There is no difference here in how it worked in my Action! library.

It’s noteworthy to mention that each of the interactive gadgets will return special values if ESC or TAB are pressed. This enables you to write a looping routing to process different input gadget elements as a form and allow the user to TAB through each control – much like modern GUI’s. In the demo program here you can see the handling of these keys which prevent the exit of the loop/form until both sets of radio buttons have the 3rd option selected.

Here is the demo program showing how the function is called for both orientations.

// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Program: gradio.c
// Desc...: A8 Library Gadget Radio Button Test
// Author.: Ripdubski
// Date...: 202208
// Notes..: cl65 -v [-O] -t atarixl gradio.c -o gradio.xex
// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Pull in include files
#include <stdio.h>
#include <conio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <atari.h>

#include "a8defines.h"
#include "a8defwin.h"
#include "a8libmisc.c"
#include "a8libstr.c"
#include "a8libwin.c"
#include "a8libgadg.c"
#include "a8libmenu.c"


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void DoRadio(void)
// Desc...: Displays radio button demo
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void DoRadio(void)
{
    byte bW, bra, brb, brap, brbp;
    unsigned char *rA[4] = { "One", "Two", "Three" };
    unsigned char *rB[4] = { "Choice A", "Choice B", "Choice C" };

    // Set defaults for selection and previous selection
    bra = 1;
    brb = 1;
    brap = bra;
    brbp = brb;

    // Open window
    bW = WOpen(2, 5, 36, 12, WOFF);
    WOrn(bW, WPTOP, WPLFT, "Radio Button Test");

    // Show horizontal buttons
    WPrint(bW, 1, 2, WOFF, "Radio Buttons (horiz)");
    GRadio(bW, 2, 3, GHORZ, GDISP, brap, 3, rA);

    // Show veritcal buttons
    WPrint(bW, 1, 5, WOFF, "Radio Buttons (vert)");
    GRadio(bW, 2, 6, GVERT, GDISP, brbp, 3, rB);

    WPrint(bW, 1, 10, WOFF, "Pick 3rd choices to exit.");

    // Loop until accepted or cancelled
    while ((bra != 3) || (brb != 3))
    {
        // Display horiz buttons and get choice
        bra = GRadio(bW, 2, 3, GHORZ, GEDIT, brap, 3, rA);

        // If not bypass, set previous selected value
        if ((bra != XESC) && (bra != XTAB))
        {
            brap = bra;
        }

        // Redisplay buttons
        GRadio(bW, 2, 3, GHORZ, GDISP, brap, 3, rA);

        // Display vert buttons and get choice
        brb = GRadio(bW, 2, 6, GVERT, GEDIT, brbp, 3, rB);

        // If not bypass, set previous selected value
        if ((brb != XESC) && (brb != XTAB))
        {
            brbp = brb;
        }

        // Redisplay buttons
        GRadio(bW, 2, 6, GVERT, GDISP, brbp, 3, rB);
    }

    // Close window
    WClose(bW);
}


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void main(void)
// Desc...: Main routine
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void main(void)
{
    // Variables
    byte bW1, bW2, bC, bD;
    unsigned char *pcM[7] = { " Alert        ", " Progress     ", " Buttons      ", " Checkboxes   ", " Radio Button ", " Quit         " };

    // Setup screen
    WInit();
    WBack(14);

    // Open windows
    bW1 = WOpen(0, 0, 40, 3, WON);
    WPrint(bW1, WPCNT, 1, WOFF, "CC65 A8 Library Test");
    bW2 = WOpen(12, 6, 16, 10, WOFF);
    WOrn(bW2, WPTOP, WPCNT, "Menu");

    // Set done to false
    bD = FALSE;

    // Loop until done (Quit selected)
    while (! bD) {
        // Call menu
        bC = MenuV(bW2, 1, 2, WON, 1, 6, pcM);

        // Process choice
        switch (bC)
        {
            case 1: GAlert("Alert! menu option 1 selected!");
                    break;

            case 2: GAlert("Alert! menu option 2 selected!");
                    break;

            case 3: GAlert("Alert! menu option 3 selected!");
                    break;

            case 4: GAlert("Alert! menu option 4 selected!");
                    break;

            case 5: DoRadio();
                    break;

            case 6: bD = TRUE;
                    break;
        }
    }

    // Wait for a keypress
    while (! kbhit()) { };

    // Close windows
    WClose(bW2);
    WClose(bW1);

    // Exit
    return;
}

Here is a screenshot of it in use:

item.title: C Style! – Action! Library Conversion 6
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: In the previous post I converted the non-interactive gadget GProc(). In this post I move to the most basic of user interaction: buttons (like OK, Cancel) via function GButton(). The button labels and quantity are up to you, as well as the default button selection. The programmer is required to specify any ornaments for the […]
item.content:encoded:
In the previous post I converted the non-interactive gadget GProc(). In this post I move to the most basic of user interaction: buttons (like OK, Cancel) via function GButton(). The button labels and quantity are up to you, as well as the default button selection.

The programmer is required to specify any ornaments for the buttons, if so desired. As the user moves between buttons, the active one is displayed in inverse video. The implementation in C is actually quite simpler than an Action! due to C’s advanced handling of arrays of strings. The source code will be released once the entire library has been converted – at that point you can compare if you are so inclined.

Here is a program demonstrating its use:

// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Program: gbutton.c
// Desc...: A8 Library Gadget Button Test
// Author.: Ripdubski
// Date...: 202208
// Notes..: cl65 -v [-O] -t atarixl gbutton.c -o gbutton.xex
// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Pull in include files
#include <stdio.h>
#include <conio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <atari.h>

#include "a8defines.h"
#include "a8defwin.h"
#include "a8libmisc.c"
#include "a8libstr.c"
#include "a8libwin.c"
#include "a8libgadg.c"
#include "a8libmenu.c"


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void DoButt(void)
// Desc...: Displays button demo
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void DoButt(void)
{
    byte bC, bW;
    unsigned char *cS[3] = { "[ Ok ]", "[ Cancel ]" };

    // Open window
    bW = WOpen(9, 10, 22, 6, WOFF);
    WOrn(bW, WPTOP, WPLFT, "Buttons");
    WPrint(bW, 2, 2, WOFF, "Select a button:");

    // Call the button handler with all buttons defined
    bC = GButton(bW, 5, 4, 2, 2, cS);

    // Close window
    WClose(bW);

    return;
}


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void main(void)
// Desc...: Main routine
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void main(void)
{
    // Variables
    byte bW1, bW2, bC, bD;
    unsigned char *pcM[7] = { " Alert        ", " Progress     ", " Buttons      ", " Checkboxes   ", " Radio Button ", " Quit         " };

    // Setup screen
    WInit();
    WBack(14);

    // Open windows
    bW1 = WOpen(0, 0, 40, 3, WON);
    WPrint(bW1, WPCNT, 1, WOFF, "CC65 A8 Library Test");
    bW2 = WOpen(12, 6, 16, 10, WOFF);
    WOrn(bW2, WPTOP, WPCNT, "Menu");

    // Set done to false
    bD = FALSE;

    // Loop until done (Quit selected)
    while (! bD) {
        // Call menu
        bC = MenuV(bW2, 1, 2, WON, 1, 6, pcM);

        // Process choice
        switch (bC)
        {
            case 1: GAlert("Alert! menu option 1 selected!");
                    break;

            case 2: GAlert("Alert! menu option 2 selected!");
                    break;

            case 3: DoButt();
                    break;

            case 4: GAlert("Alert! menu option 4 selected!");
                    break;

            case 5: GAlert("Alert! menu option 5 selected!");
                    break;

            case 6: bD = TRUE;
                    break;
        }
    }

    // Wait for a keypress
    while (! kbhit()) { };

    // Close windows
    WClose(bW2);
    WClose(bW1);

    // Exit
    return;
}

Here is a screenshot of it running:

item.title: C Style! – Action! Library Conversion 5
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: In the next set of posts I’ll be converting my Action! libraries Gadget routines. Gadgets are what I refer to as the widgets used in conjunction with the windowing library to create form based input. Think of a gadget as an input mechanism like a button (OK, Cancel, etc), a radio button (where only 1 […]
item.content:encoded:
In the next set of posts I’ll be converting my Action! libraries Gadget routines. Gadgets are what I refer to as the widgets used in conjunction with the windowing library to create form based input. Think of a gadget as an input mechanism like a button (OK, Cancel, etc), a radio button (where only 1 over several choices can be selected), a checkbox, etc. The first one I converted is not so much as an input control, but a way to show progress to a user during a slow operation. This is the GProg() function.

This function is called with the placement location within an already opened window handle, and the value (0 to 100) to display.

Here is the code that demonstrates:

// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Program: gprog.c
// Desc...: A8 Library Progress Gadget Test
// Author.: Ripdubski
// Date...: 202208
// Notes..: cl65 -v [-O] -t atarixl gprog.c -o gprog.xex
// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Pull in include files
#include <stdio.h>
#include <conio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <atari.h>

#include "a8defines.h"
#include "a8defwin.h"
#include "a8libmisc.c"
#include "a8libstr.c"
#include "a8libwin.c"
#include "a8libgadg.c"
#include "a8libmenu.c"


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void DoProg(void)
// Desc...: Displays progress bar demo
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void DoProg(void)
{
    byte bL, bW;

    // Open window
    bW = WOpen(9, 10, 22, 4, WOFF);
    WOrn(bW, WPTOP, WPLFT, "Progress");

    // Loop through progress
    for (bL=1; bL <= 100; bL+=5)
    {
        // Display the progress bar and wait 1 second
        GProg(bW, 1, 2, bL);
        sleep(1);
    }

    // Close window
    WClose(bW);

    return;
}


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void main(void)
// Desc...: Main routine
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void main(void)
{
    // Variables
    byte bW1, bW2, bC, bD;
    unsigned char *pcM[7] = { " Alert        ", " Progress     ", " Buttons      ", " Checkboxes   ", " Radio Button ", " Quit         " };

    // Setup screen
    WInit();
    WBack(14);

    // Open windows
    bW1 = WOpen(0, 0, 40, 3, WON);
    WPrint(bW1, WPCNT, 1, WOFF, "CC65 A8 Library Test");
    bW2 = WOpen(12, 6, 16, 10, WOFF);
    WOrn(bW2, WPTOP, WPCNT, "Menu");

    // Set done to false
    bD = FALSE;

    // Loop until done (Quit selected)
    while (! bD) {
        // Call menu
        bC = MenuV(bW2, 1, 2, WON, 1, 6, pcM);

        // Process choice
        switch (bC)
        {
            case 1: GAlert("Alert! menu option 1 selected!");
                    break;

            case 2: DoProg();
                    break;

            case 3: GAlert("Alert! menu option 3 selected!");
                    break;

            case 4: GAlert("Alert! menu option 4 selected!");
                    break;

            case 5: GAlert("Alert! menu option 5 selected!");
                    break;

            case 6: bD = TRUE;
                    break;
        }
    }

    // Wait for a keypress
    while (! kbhit()) { };

    // Close windows
    WClose(bW2);
    WClose(bW1);

    // Exit
    return;
}

Here is a screenshot showing what it looks like.

item.title: Action! Library v1.51
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: (edit: license change, new atr, and github link) As I worked on converting my Action! library to C for use with CC65, I stumbled on a few typos which resulted in minor bugs. This release addresses those and provides a minor update to the manual as well. This version supercedes all previous versions. The attached […]
item.content:encoded:
(edit: license change, new atr, and github link)

As I worked on converting my Action! library to C for use with CC65, I stumbled on a few typos which resulted in minor bugs. This release addresses those and provides a minor update to the manual as well. This version supercedes all previous versions.

The attached file has a “.odt” extension due to upload restrictions. Simply download it, rename it so “.ATR” is the only extension. The API documentation is in PDF format.

Version 1.6 will include the FujiNet and SQL bindings. I do not have an ETA on that release yet.

Downloads

You can find the library here:

https://unfinishedbitness.info/atari-8-bit-action-library/

Enjoy.

item.title: C Style! – Action! Library Conversion 4
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
item.description: With the core Action! Library window routines complete, I moved to converting the menu routine, and the first gadget. The menu routine converted is MenuV(), which is a vertical stacked menu. The first gadget to be converted is GAlert() which simply displays a message in a window and waits for a keypress. As a reminder […]
item.content:encoded:
With the core Action! Library window routines complete, I moved to converting the menu routine, and the first gadget. The menu routine converted is MenuV(), which is a vertical stacked menu. The first gadget to be converted is GAlert() which simply displays a message in a window and waits for a keypress.

As a reminder the actual library source code is being withheld until the conversion is completed. Here is the source for the sample program which demonstrates calling the routines. The command line used to compile is noted in the header comment:

// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Program: menuv.c
// Desc...: A8 Library Menu and Alert Test
// Author.: Ripdubski
// Date...: 202208
// Notes..: cl65 -v [-O] -t atarixl menuv.c -o menuv.xex
// ------------------------------------------------------------

// Pull in include files
#include <stdio.h>
#include <conio.h>
#include <unistd.h>
#include <string.h>
#include <atari.h>

// Converted Action! library files
// Redacted until full conversion complete.
#include "a8defines.h"
#include "a8defwin.h"
#include "a8libmisc.c"
#include "a8libstr.c"
#include "a8libwin.c"
#include "a8libgadg.c"
#include "a8libmenu.c"


// ------------------------------------------------------------
// Func...: void main(void)
// Desc...: Main routine
// ------------------------------------------------------------
void main(void)
{
    // Variables
    byte bW1, bW2, bC, bD;
    unsigned char *pcM[7] = { " Alert        ", " Progress     ", " Buttons      ", " Checkboxes   ", " Radio Button ", " Quit         " };

    // Setup screen
    WInit();
    WBack(14);

    // Open windows
    bW1 = WOpen(0, 0, 40, 3, WON);
    WPrint(bW1, WPCNT, 1, WOFF, "CC65 A8 Menu Test");
    bW2 = WOpen(12, 6, 16, 10, WOFF);
    WOrn(bW2, WPTOP, WPCNT, "Menu");

    // Set done to false
    bD = FALSE;

    // Loop until done (Quit selected)
    while (! bD) 
    {
        // Call menu
        bC = MenuV(bW2, 1, 2, WON, 1, 6, pcM);

        // Process choice
        switch (bC)
        {
            // For each option, show an alert using the GAlert gadget.
            case 1: GAlert("Alert! menu option 1 selected!");
                    break;

            case 2: GAlert("Alert! menu option 2 selected!");
                    break;

            case 3: GAlert("Alert! menu option 3 selected!");
                    break;

            case 4: GAlert("Alert! menu option 4 selected!");
                    break;

            case 5: GAlert("Alert! menu option 5 selected!");
                    break;

            // Quit option, set exit to true
            case 6: bD = TRUE;
                    break;
        }
    }

    // Wait for a keypress
    while (! kbhit()) { };

    // Close windows
    WClose(bW2);
    WClose(bW1);

    // Exit
    return;
}

Screenshot of the MenuV() function being used:

Screenshot of the GAlert() gadget being used:

item.title: C Style! – Action! Library Conversion 3
item.dc:creator: Ripdubski
